chunk_summary: This section discusses protein requirements across childhood, emphasizing age-specific daily targets, protein quality, and dietary counseling for growth, obesity, and clinical exceptions like preterm infants and chronic kidney disease.
learning_objectives: Understand age-specific protein requirements for children and adolescents.
Learn the importance of protein quality and strategies to improve amino acid profiles in diets.
Recognize clinical scenarios requiring adjusted protein intake, such as preterm infants or chronic kidney disease.
Identify dietary counseling strategies for growth faltering and obesity management.
key_facts: Daily protein requirements: 0–6 months: 8 g, 6–12 months: 10.5 g, 1–3 years: 12.5 g, 4–6 years: 16 g, 7–9 years: 23 g, 10–12 years: ~32 g, late adolescence: 46 g/day (girls), 55 g/day (boys).
Convert protein intake to g/kg/day based on the child’s weight to avoid over- or under-prescription.
High-quality protein sources include milk, curd, paneer, egg, fish, chicken, or cereal-pulse combinations with dairy for vegetarians.
In growth faltering, prioritize balanced energy and protein intake over protein powders.
In obesity, maintain protein intake while reducing free sugars and ultra-processed snacks.
Higher protein density is required for preterm infants and during catch-up growth after illness.
reference_quote: The ICMR-NIN 2020 brief note provides practical daily targets: 0–6 months 8 g, 6–12 months 10.5 g, 1–3 years 12.5 g, 4–6 years 16 g, 7–9 years 23 g, 10–12 years about 32 g, and in late adolescence about 46 g/day for girls and 55 g/day for boys.
document_key_topics: Dietary counseling for growth and obesity
